title: Fundamentals of Competition Law
content:
Session 1: Competition Law ‚Äď Fundamentals 
CCI v Co-ordination Committee of Artists and Technicians of WB Film and Television and Others [2017 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Informant: Sajjan Kumar Khaitan, proprietor of Hart Video ‚Üí held rights from BRTV to dub Hindi serial Mahabharat 
into Bangla for distribution/telecasting. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Channels involved: Channel 10 (Bengal Media Pvt. Ltd.); CTVN+ (Calcutta Television Network Pvt. Ltd.) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Both entered revenue-sharing deals with Hart Video to broadcast the Bangla-dubbed serial. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Opposing Industry Bodies: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Co-ordination Committee ‚Äď joint platform of 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Federation of Senior Technicians & Workers of Eastern India 
‚Ė†‚Äč
West Bengal Motion Picture Artistes‚Äô Forum ‚Üí Though labelled a "committee," it acted on behalf of 
producers, distributors, technicians, artists‚ÄĒi.e., industry constituents. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
EIMPA ‚Äď Eastern India Motion Picture Association (producers, distributors, exhibitors). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Events ‚Üí Telecast advertisements for the dubbed Mahabharat were published by Channel 10 and CTVN+. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Co-ordination Committee and EIMPA issued threats demanding stoppage of the telecast. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Warnings of ‚Äúnon-cooperation‚ÄĚ from artists, technicians, producers, exhibitors if telecast continued. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč BUT ‚Üí CTVN+ continued telecasting; Channel 10 capitulated‚ÄĒstopped broadcasting due to pressure. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Informant approached CCI alleging anti-competitive conduct under Section 3 of the Competition Act. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Procedural History 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Majority: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
DG investigation confirmed threats, boycotts, work-stoppage calls. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Held that both bodies limited/controlled supply of dubbed serial ‚Üí violation of Section 3(3)(b). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Rejected ‚Äútrade union‚ÄĚ defence ‚Üí held the bodies were associations of enterprises, not mere unions. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Minority/Judicial Member: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Said relevant market = TV broadcasting only. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Coordination Committee not economically active in that market; acted as a trade union; no ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ 
among competing enterprises. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč COMPAT accepted minority view; set aside CCI majority. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Appeal to SC. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Relevant Market Determination ‚Äď should the market be confined to ‚ÄúTV broadcasting of serials‚ÄĚ? Or the broader ‚Äúfilm and 
TV industry in West Bengal‚ÄĚ? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the Co-ordination Committee is an ‚Äúenterprise‚ÄĚ or an ‚Äúassociation of enterprises‚ÄĚ under Section 2(h) and whether 
its conduct constitutes economic activity. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the boycott threats and coordinated conduct constitute an ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ under Section 2(b) read with Section 3. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether conduct amounts to limiting or controlling supply of dubbed TV content. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether Article 19 protected ‚Äútrade union protest‚ÄĚ immunises the conduct from competition law. 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 3(1): Prohibits agreements between enterprises/persons that cause or are likely to cause AAEC. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 3(3)(b): Presumes AAEC where agreement/decision results in limiting or controlling supply of goods or services. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Definition of ‚Äúenterprise‚ÄĚ ‚Äď Section 2(h): Includes any entity engaged in economic activity, regardless of profit motive or 
legal form. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Definition of ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ ‚Äď Section 2(b): includes any arrangement, understanding, or even action in concert; oral or 
informal; enforceability irrelevant. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Competition law principle: Enterprise status depends on functional/economic activity, not form. 
79 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Trade union exemption: Exists only when acting solely as collective bargaining agent, not when engaged in market-affecting 
conduct backed by enterprises. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Carving out RM: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Demand-side substitutability 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Primary Test: SSNIP test ‚Üí ‚ÄúSmall but Significant Non-Transitory Increase in Price‚ÄĚ 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Typically 5‚Äď10% permanent price increase. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
If consumers shift from Product X to Product Y in response ‚Üí X and Y are in the same relevant 
market. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Criticisms of SSNIP: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Digital markets problem 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Many platforms are free (zero-price markets). 
‚óÜ‚Äč
SSNIP cannot operate when price = zero. 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Non-price factors (data, attention) matter more. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Imperfect substitutes 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Depending on preferences, even imperfect substitutes may belong to the same market. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Cellophane Fallacy 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Origin: U.S. v. DuPont (Cellophane case). 
‚óÜ‚Äč
If a monopolist has already set monopolistic prices, small increases lead consumers to 
any cheaper alternative, even poor substitutes. 
‚óÜ‚Äč
This can mistakenly suggest that the monopolist faces competitive constraints. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Supply-side substitutability 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Meaning ‚Üí can another supplier switch production quickly and fill the gap if one firm raises prices? 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Requires: 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Switching ability 
‚óÜ‚Äč
No major regulatory/technological barriers 
‚óÜ‚Äč
A short time frame 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Relevant Market 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Geographic Market ‚Äď not disputed to be WB. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Product Market 
‚Ė†‚Äč
COMPAT/minority: Only ‚ÄúTV broadcasting‚ÄĚ market. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
SC found this myopic and disregarding industry-wide effects. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Letters issued by the Committee themselves admitted that dubbed content harms the entire TV 
and film industry of WB. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Therefore, the effect of the boycott was not confined to two channels or broadcasting alone. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Proper market: Film and Television Industry in WB, including distribution, exhibition, content 
production. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
SC used the Committee‚Äôs own admission to define the market. 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Since CCI proceedings are not strictly adversarial, relying on such admission is valid. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the Coordination Committee is an ‚Äúenterprise‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Functional Test 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Enterprise = entity performing economic activity ‚Üí profit motive irrelevant ‚áí non-profit/public bodies 
can be enterprises when they engage in market activity. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Application 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The Coordination Committee itself does not engage in economic activity (not producing/supplying 
goods/services) BUT its constituent members (producers, distributors, exhibitors, technicians, artists) do 
‚Üí operate as an association of enterprises, not merely a trade union. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Why trade union defence fails 
80 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč If acting purely in trade union capacity ‚Üí not an enterprise. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
BUT HERE: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Coordinated boycotts on behalf of producers/distributors/exhibitors. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Acted jointly with EIMPA. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Decisions reflected the collective intent of economically active members. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Therefore, treatable as ‚Äúassociation of enterprises‚ÄĚ under Section 3. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Articles 19(1)(c) and 19(1)(g) do not permit conduct that distorts markets ‚Üí cannot collude to influence markets 
on behalf of producer/distributor members. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether an ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ existed 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 2(b) standard ‚Üí extremely broad; includes informal, oral, tacit coordination. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč HERE, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Letters, threats, work-stoppage calls clearly show concerted action. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Joint action of EIMPA and Coordination Committee = agreement. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Purpose: stop telecast of dubbed serial ‚Üí limit market access. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, agreement was established. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Violation under Section 3(3)(b) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Conduct 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Threatened boycott of channels. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Pressured them to stop telecasting. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Channel 10 complied; telecast was halted. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Effects 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Limited supply of Bangla-dubbed content. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Foreclosed entry of dubbed serials into the West Bengal market. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Reduced consumer choice, harming viewers. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Protected incumbent local content producers under guise of preserving Bengali language. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Under Section 3(3)(b), such limitation automatically presumes AAEC ‚Üí clear violation. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč SC restored CCI‚Äôs majority decision and overturned COMPAT ‚Üí held that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Relevant market = Film and Television Industry in West Bengal. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Coordination Committee = association of enterprises, not merely a trade union. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Their boycott calls constitute an agreement under Section 3. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Conduct limited supply, restricted competition, and harmed consumers. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Violation under Section 3(3)(b) established. 
 
CCI v State of Mizoram [2022 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Under the Lotteries (Regulation) Act, the State of Mizoram conducts state lotteries. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč For implementation, the State issued an Expression of Interest (EoI) in 2011 to appoint lottery distributors and 
selling agents (i.e., private entities who would run the scheme operationally) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Four private companies submitted bids ‚Äď quoted exactly the minimum allowable rate, raising suspicion of: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Collusive bidding 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Bid rigging cartel under Section 3(3)(d) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč A losing bidder filed information before the CCI alleging: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Collusion among the four private bidders 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Abuse of dominance by the State of Mizoram in the lottery market 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI‚Äôs prima facie order found evidence of possible cartelisation and ordered the DG to investigate. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Also held that the State of Mizoram is NOT an ‚Äúenterprise‚ÄĚ under Section 2(h) SO ‚Üí Section 4 cannot apply to 
the State. 
81 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč But DG may investigate private parties for cartelisation. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč State‚Äôs Challenge in High Court 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Despite the CCI effectively dropping the case against the State, Mizoram filed a writ petition, seeking to quash the 
DG‚Äôs adverse observations. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Gauhati High Court held: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Lotteries are res extra commercium (outside commerce) ‚Üí the Competition Act does not apply. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI lacked jurisdiction to investigate even bid rigging. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Appeal to Supreme Court 
 
Issue: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Does CCI have jurisdiction to investigate bid rigging in state lotteries, a regulated activity? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Are lotteries, being res extra commercium, outside the scope of the Competition Act? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether distribution/sale of lottery tickets is a ‚Äúservice‚ÄĚ under Section 2(u) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Do selling agents provide a service to purchasers (potential users)? 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Competition Act Provisions 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(1): prohibits anti-competitive agreements. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(3): presumption of AAEC for certain forms of collusion, including bid rigging. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 2(u) ‚Äď ‚ÄúService‚ÄĚ: ‚ÄúService of any description‚ÄĚ made available to potential users; inclusive definition. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Doctrines & Principles 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Res extra commercium: Some activities (like gambling/lotteries) are outside ‚Äútrade/commerce,‚ÄĚ limiting 
protections under Articles 19(1)(g) ‚Üí BUT ‚Üí does not immunise activities from statutory regulatory schemes 
unless expressly excluded. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Jurisdictional coexistence: Multiple regulatory regimes can apply to the same economic activity unless there is 
irreconcilable conflict. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Enterprise (Section 2(h)): Entity engaged in economic activity other than sovereign functions. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 1 ‚Äď CCI's Jurisdiction in Lottery Sector ‚Äď YES. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI‚Äôs concern is not regulation or prohibition of lotteries ‚Üí its concern is ONLY whether bid rigging occurred in 
the tendering process. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No conflict exists between: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Lotteries (Regulation) Act 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Competition Act 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Both can apply simultaneously because: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Lottery regulation = public law supervision 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Bid rigging = market competition issue 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Why High Court was wrong 
‚Ė†‚Äč
HC misunderstood the scope: it thought CCI was questioning the lottery scheme itself BUT ‚Üí CCI was 
not at all concerned with carrying out, regulating, or prohibiting the lottery business, only with 
examining any perceived bid rigging in the tendering process. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Regulated sectors are NOT immune from competition law. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Even gambling/lotteries can involve markets in: 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Procurement 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Distribution 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Commission-based operations 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 2 ‚Äď Whether lotteries being res extra commercium bars Competition Act ‚Äď NO 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The doctrine only limits constitutional protection for business‚ÄĒnot statutory oversight. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SO ‚Üí even if the lottery product is res extra commercium: 
82 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The process of selecting vendors is an economic activity. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The State, once choosing to engage in lottery operations, cannot shield the associated commercial 
procurement markets from competition scrutiny. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SC essentially decouples the ‚Äúlottery product‚ÄĚ from the ‚Äúmarket for lottery distribution services‚ÄĚ ‚Üí allows the 
Competition Act to apply. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 3 ‚Äď Whether sale/distribution of lottery tickets = ‚Äúservice‚ÄĚ (Section 2(u)) ‚Äď YES 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 2(u) is expansive ‚Üí covers ‚Äúservice of any description.‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A lottery purchaser is a potential user + selling agents make a service ‚Äúavailable.‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus the Competition Act clearly applies. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Distribution & sale = economic activity 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Agents = service providers 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI has full jurisdiction to investigate bid rigging in lottery tenders. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Res extra commercium is NO bar to Competition Act. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Distribution/sale of lottery tickets is a ‚Äúservice‚ÄĚ under Section 2(u). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Tendering for lottery operations involves economic activity, even if the underlying lottery product is regulated. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The High Court erred in blocking CCI‚Äôs investigation. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Outcome 
‚ěĘ‚Äč DG investigation revived. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI was allowed to proceed against private bidders. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč State not liable under Section 4 (since CCI had originally excluded it). 
 
Comments: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The judgment is widely criticised for two omissions: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No analysis of ‚Äúenterprise‚ÄĚ definition (Section 2(h)) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
State of Mizoram awarded licences through a tender ‚Üí SC skipped the enterprise analysis entirely. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
It did not examine: Whether the State acted in sovereign capacity (lottery = public function?) Whether 
tendering for distribution is a commercial activity sufficient for enterprise status. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč No engagement with ‚Äúsovereign function exception‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 2(h) excludes sovereign functions. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Running lotteries arguably involves public revenue and statutory authority. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SC avoids articulating: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Why tendering for lottery agents is NOT sovereign. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Whether outsourcing a sovereign function to private actors converts it into commercial activity. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Thus, the judgment remains: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Correct outcome (CCI may investigate bid rigging) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Doctrinally weak on enterprise jurisprudence. 
 
 
Beach Mineral Producers Association v Government of India [2025 | NCLAT | 2 M]  
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Beach Sand Minerals (BSMs) found in five coastal states; include ilmenite, rutile, garnet, zircon, sillimanite, leucoxene and 
monazite. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Earlier, the Atomic Energy Act restricted BSM use to atomic purposes ‚Üí only IREL and KMML could mine/process. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Post-1998 liberalisation allowed private entry and FDI (74% in 1998; up to 100% for titanium minerals in 2008). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Private sector output grew sharply; exports rose from Rs 35 crore (1997‚Äď98) to about Rs 4000 crore (2017‚Äď18). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč 2018 Policy Change 
‚ěĘ‚Äč DGFT‚Äôs 21.08.2018 notification (under FTDR Act/FTP) shifted all BSM exports to a State Trading Enterprise 
(STE) system and made IREL the sole canalising agency. An SOP for exports was also issued. 
83 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Informants‚Äô Claims ‚Üí the notification allegedly gave IREL a dominant position and excluded private exporters, violating 
Section 4 of the Competition Act. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI‚Äôs Decision (Section 26(2)) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI held BSMs are strategic/atomic minerals and STE designation is a policy choice. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč DGFT‚Äôs policy and IREL‚Äôs implementation fall outside Section 4 review. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No contravention found; case closed. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Does the 2018 DGFT Notification foreclose private exporters and violate Competition Act? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Are DGFT (OP-1/OP-2) and IREL (OP-3) ‚Äúenterprises‚ÄĚ under Section 2(h) of the Competition Act? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Does the designation of OP-3 as a State Trading Enterprise amount to abuse of dominant position under Section 4? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Can CCI review government policy decisions under the FTDR Act and Atomic Energy framework? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Was CCI justified in closing the information under Section 26(2)? 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Competition Act, 2002 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 4(1): No enterprise or group shall abuse its dominant position. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 2(h): Definition of ‚ÄúEnterprise‚ÄĚ ‚Üí Includes a person or department of the Government engaged in 
production, supply, distribution, acquisition, control of goods/services; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Excludes: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Any activity of the Government relatable to sovereign functions, including activities of departments 
dealing with: atomic energy, defence, currency, space. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Thus, activities connected with atomic minerals/prescribed substances fall outside the purview of 
‚Äúenterprise.‚ÄĚ 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Other Statutory Frameworks 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FTDR Act, 1992 ‚ÄĒ Section 3: empowers the government to issue export-policy notifications. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FTP 2015‚Äď2020 ‚ÄĒ Paras 1.02, 2.01: policy and procedural basis for export regulations. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Atomic Energy Act, 1962 ‚ÄĒ ‚ÄúPrescribed Substances‚ÄĚ includes beach sand minerals. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč MMDR Act, 1957 ‚ÄĒ First Schedule, Part B: Atomic minerals. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether Notification forecloses private exporters? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT held: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The Notification does not bar appellants from trading with foreign buyers. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
It only requires routing exports through the designated STE (IREL) due to the strategic nature of 
minerals. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The restriction is regulatory, not anti-competitive in nature. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, no foreclosure in the competition sense. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Are DGFT & IREL ‚Äúenterprises‚ÄĚ? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč DGFT (OP-1 & OP-2) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
It formulates and implements foreign trade policy. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Functions are policy-making, statutory, and sovereign. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Falls squarely within the sovereign functions exclusion of Section 2(h). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč IREL (OP-3) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Though a PSU, in this context it acts pursuant to a government policy regulating export of 
prescribed/atomic minerals. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Such activities are also tied to sovereign objectives (atomic energy, strategic materials). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Therefore, even OP-3‚Äôs impugned actions are implementation of sovereign policy, not market behaviour. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Hence, none of the OPs fit within ‚Äúenterprise‚ÄĚ for purposes of Section 4. 
84 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Does DGFT/IREL abuse dominance? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 4 only applies to ‚Äúenterprises.‚ÄĚ 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Since the DGFT and IREL‚Äôs activities in this context are sovereign/statutory, Section 4 cannot apply. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI correctly held: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Changes in export policy, formulated under FTDR Act, cannot be challenged as abuse of dominance. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
IREL‚Äôs role is mandated by law. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore, no abuse of dominance is possible. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Can CCI review policy decisions? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT affirmed that the challenge essentially seeks to invalidate a government notification issued under FTDR 
Act. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CCI/NCLAT is not the forum for reviewing or quashing trade policy. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Such relief lies ‚Äúelsewhere‚ÄĚ (i.e., High Court under writ jurisdiction), not via competition proceedings. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, CCI acted properly. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Was CCI justified in closing under Section 26(2)? Yes. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Since: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Policy decisions concerning strategic minerals 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Activities relating to atomic energy 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Statutory actions under FTDR Act 
‚ěĘ‚Äč do not fall within anti-competitive conduct under Section 4 ‚Üí there is no prima facie case. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Hence, Section 26(2) closure was appropriate. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Appeal dismissed. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCI‚Äôs order upheld; no interference required. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč OPs‚Äô actions arise from statutory/sovereign functions linked to atomic minerals and strategic sectors. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč DGFT (policy-making body) and IREL (STE under atomic energy framework) are not ‚Äúenterprises‚ÄĚ for purposes of Section 
4. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Export policy changes cannot be scrutinised under the Competition Act. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Appellants may pursue remedies in appropriate forums (e.g., constitutional courts) if they seek quashing of the 2018 
Notification. 
 
Travel Agents Association of India  v CCI [2024 | NCLAT] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Travel Agents Association of India (TAAI) challenged an order of the CCI. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč TAAI alleged that the Department of Expenditure (DOE), along with Balmer Lawrie and Ashok Travels, engaged 
in anti-competitive conduct. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč DOE had issued Office Memorandum (OM1) in 2006 (and updated versions) requiring that all central government air ticket 
bookings be routed only through Balmer Lawrie and Ashok Travels. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč TAAI argued this foreclosed the market to private travel agents and amounted to bid rigging / refusal to deal. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč A similar complaint in 2010 was dismissed by the CCI and upheld on appeal. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč In 2020, TAAI filed another complaint, claiming circumstances had evolved ‚Üí but the allegations remained substantially 
identical. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether DOE is an ‚Äúenterprise‚ÄĚ under Section 2(h) of the Competition Act. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the Office Memorandum (OM1) constitutes an ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ under Section 3. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether DOE‚Äôs actions amount to refusal to deal under Section 3(4). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the second complaint is barred by res judicata. 
 
85 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč DOE is not an ‚Äúenterprise‚ÄĚ under Section 2(h) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč DOE's functions are administrative, regulatory, and policy-making, not commercial. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč It oversees: public financial management, central/state finances, and implementation of Pay Commission 
recommendations. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
DOE‚Äôs activities are in the realm of policy making and not commercial in nature. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, DOE is not an enterprise, at least in relation to the impugned circulars. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč OM1 is not an ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ under Section 3(4) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3(4) requires a vertical relationship between two ‚Äúenterprises‚ÄĚ (e.g., manufacturer‚Äďdistributor). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
DOE does not operate in any market level of air travel services + there does not seem to be any vertical 
relationship between DOE and Balmer Lawrie/Ashok Travels. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Therefore: no vertical relationship, no vertical agreement, and no ‚Äúrefusal to deal‚ÄĚ under Section 3(4) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč OM1 is also not an ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ under Section 3(1) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 3(1) requires an economic arrangement or concerted conduct. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
But OM1 is merely an internal administrative circular dictating how the Government wishes to procure 
its own tickets. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
It is a policy choice, not a market agreement. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, no contravention of Section 3(1). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Res Judicata applies 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The same issue was litigated in 2010 ‚Üí dismissed by CCI ‚Üí upheld by COMPAT ‚Üí not appealed to SC. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
TAAI‚Äôs 2020 complaint was materially identical. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Therefore, barred by res judicata.
cases: Beach Mineral Producers Association v Government of India
Travel Agents Association of India  v CCI
key_principles: ‚Äč Informant: Sajjan Kumar Khaitan, proprietor of Hart Video ‚Üí held rights from BRTV to dub Hindi serial Mahabharat
‚Äč Channels involved: Channel 10 (Bengal Media Pvt. Ltd.); CTVN+ (Calcutta Television Network Pvt. Ltd.)
‚Äč Both entered revenue-sharing deals with Hart Video to broadcast the Bangla-dubbed serial.
‚Äč Opposing Industry Bodies:
‚Äč Co-ordination Committee ‚Äď joint platform of
‚Äč Events ‚Üí Telecast advertisements for the dubbed Mahabharat were published by Channel 10 and CTVN+.
‚Äč The Co-ordination Committee and EIMPA issued threats demanding stoppage of the telecast.
‚Äč Warnings of ‚Äúnon-cooperation‚ÄĚ from artists, technicians, producers, exhibitors if telecast continued.
‚Äč BUT ‚Üí CTVN+ continued telecasting; Channel 10 capitulated‚ÄĒstopped broadcasting due to pressure.
‚Äč Informant approached CCI alleging anti-competitive conduct under Section 3 of the Competition Act.
‚Äč Procedural History
‚Äč Minority/Judicial Member:
‚Äč COMPAT accepted minority view; set aside CCI majority.
‚Äč Relevant Market Determination ‚Äď should the market be confined to ‚ÄúTV broadcasting of serials‚ÄĚ? Or the broader ‚Äúfilm and
‚Äč Whether the Co-ordination Committee is an ‚Äúenterprise‚ÄĚ or an ‚Äúassociation of enterprises‚ÄĚ under Section 2(h) and whether
‚Äč Whether the boycott threats and coordinated conduct constitute an ‚Äúagreement‚ÄĚ under Section 2(b) read with Section 3.
‚Äč Whether conduct amounts to limiting or controlling supply of dubbed TV content.
‚Äč Whether Article 19 protected ‚Äútrade union protest‚ÄĚ immunises the conduct from competition law.
‚Äč Section 3(1): Prohibits agreements between enterprises/persons that cause or are likely to cause AAEC.
‚Äč Section 3(3)(b): Presumes AAEC where agreement/decision results in limiting or controlling supply of goods or services.
